name: Jack Creek
short_description: BLM public land near Whitehall offering quality quartz and agate specimens in gentle mountain terrain. Free rockhounding with a 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Jack Creek sits on Montana BLM land in the Jefferson County area at 7070 feet elevation, providing accessible rockhounding for collectors of all levels. The gentle slope and open terrain make this a comfortable location to search for quartz, agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens that weather out of the surrounding rock formations. The site is particularly appealing because there are no permit requirements and collection is free - you simply need to respect the 25 lb per day personal use limit.

This area lies within the northern Rocky Mountain uplift zone where Precambrian metamorphic rocks and Tertiary-age silica-rich deposits have created ideal conditions for quality gemstone formation. The agate and chalcedony specimens here formed in ancient hydrothermal systems where silica-laden fluids filled cavities and fractures, slowly crystallizing into the banded and colorful stones rockhounds seek today.

The gentle slope at Jack Creek means you can cover ground comfortably without steep climbs, and the mix of exposed bedrock and weathered material on the surface provides natural collecting areas. Many specimens are found lying loose or partially exposed in washes and on hillsides where erosion has worked them free over millennia.

This is an ideal location for beginning rockhounders or families wanting a low-pressure introduction to Montana collecting. The lack of fees and permits removes barriers to getting out and searching, and the gem types here are common enough that most visitors will find at least a few keepers. More experienced collectors appreciate the potential to find larger specimens and unusual color combinations.
history: The Jack Creek area sits within the northern Rocky Mountain province where Precambrian metamorphic basement rocks underlie younger Tertiary volcanic and sedimentary deposits. Hydrothermal mineralization during the Tertiary epoch created silica-rich environments that precipitated the quartz, agate, and chalcedony specimens found here today. This region's geology reflects the same mountain-building processes that created Montana's famous mineral districts.
tips: Search the wash bottoms and small gullies first, as erosion naturally concentrates loose specimens in drainage areas. Look for banded agate and chalcedony pieces in the orange, brown, and cream color range - these are most common here. Use a small daypack to carry your finds and bring water, as the 7000+ foot elevation means cooler temperatures but also limited shade; a hand lens helps identify quality pieces before adding them to your 25 lb limit.
terrain_notes: Elevation 7070 feet with gentle slopes throughout the collecting area. Terrain is open with scattered low vegetation typical of Montana foothill regions. Footing is moderate - mostly stable ground with occasional loose rock and small scree patches. Walking distance from parking is typically short, within 100-300 yards of roadside pullouts. The landscape is semi-arid with sparse timber.
best_season: June - September. Higher elevation (7070 ft) means snow blocks access in winter, and spring brings unpredictable mountain weather. Summer months offer stable conditions, though afternoon thunderstorms are possible. Early June and September provide pleasant temperatures without mid-summer heat.
written_directions: From Whitehall, take MT-287 northwest toward Townsend. Continue approximately 12-15 miles on MT-287 toward the Jefferson County area. Look for BLM access points and informal pullouts on either side of the highway near Jack Creek drainage areas. The exact access will depend on which BLM section you target, but the area is well-marked on BLM maps. GPS coordinates 46.372537, -112.287249 will guide you to the primary collection area. Approach via passenger vehicle is possible on main highways; check current road conditions before visiting.
road_conditions: MT-287 is paved and well-maintained. Access roads to pullouts may be unpaved but are generally passable by standard vehicles during dry conditions.
parking_notes: Informal BLM pullouts along MT-287 and access roads. No designated parking lot. Space is limited; pull completely off the highway. Best parking is near creek drainages where access is obvious.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation equipment. Respect all marked claim boundaries and private property. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Respect claim boundaries
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Whitehall is approximately 12-15 miles south with gas, food, and lodging. Townsend is about 20 miles east with full services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers. Informal pullouts provide easy parking near collecting areas, with typically short walks from vehicle to prime spots. Unstable ground with loose rock in some areas requires sturdy footwear. Most collecting requires only walking on open terrain, making this suitable for families and older rockhounders.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Jack Creek in Montana?
faq.answer: Jack Creek is known for quartz, agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. Agate here often displays bands of orange, brown, cream, and white - colors typical of Montana deposits. Chalcedony pieces are also common. Most specimens are found loose on the surface or partially exposed in washes, making collection straightforward.

faq.question: Is there a fee to rockhound at Jack Creek, or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: No - Jack Creek sits on BLM public land with no permit requirement and no collection fee. You simply follow the standard BLM rule of 25 lbs per day for personal use. This makes it an affordable option for rockhounds wanting to get out without hassle or cost.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Jack Creek Montana?
faq.answer: June through September is ideal. The elevation of 7070 feet means snow blocks access in winter and spring brings unpredictable weather. Summer offers stable, dry conditions perfect for searching the hillsides and washes. Early June and September provide pleasant temperatures without the afternoon thunderstorms common in July and August.

faq.question: Is Jack Creek rockhounding site beginner-friendly?
faq.answer: Yes. The easy terrain with gentle slopes, lack of permit requirements, and free access make Jack Creek ideal for beginners. The gem types here are common enough that most visitors find quality specimens. The short walks from parking to collecting areas and stable footing suit families and rockhounders of all experience levels.

faq.question: How far is Jack Creek from Whitehall, and what tools should I bring?
faq.answer: Jack Creek is about 12-15 miles northwest of Whitehall via MT-287. Bring a small daypack, sturdy hiking boots, water (the high elevation and open terrain offer limited shade), a hand lens to inspect specimens, and a small hammer or rock pick. A backpack scale helps track your 25 lb daily limit.

faq.question: Are dogs allowed at Jack Creek rockhounding area?
faq.answer: Yes - Jack Creek is on BLM public land where dogs are generally permitted. Keep your dog under control, bring plenty of water for both of you (the terrain is semi-arid), and be aware of wildlife. The gentle terrain and open landscape make it a comfortable spot for bringing your pet rockhounding.

meta_title: Jack Creek Rockhounding - Montana Agate & Quartz
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Jack Creek, Montana. Find agate, quartz, and chalcedony. No permit, 25 lb limit. Beginner-friendly near Whitehall.
